content.text: Capacity testing determines the maximum throughput an agentic AI system can sustain within its SLA — the point at which adding more traffic degrades latency or increases error rates beyond acceptable thresholds. Automating capacity tests and generating structured reports transforms this from a periodic manual exercise into a repeatable, CI-integrated data source that informs scaling decisions, infrastructure provisioning, and capacity planning conversations with finance and product teams.
What it is

Capacity test automation is the practice of scripting the full lifecycle of a capacity test — environment provisioning, load execution, metric collection, threshold evaluation, and report generation — so that it runs without manual intervention and produces consistent, comparable outputs on every run. Unlike a one-time load test, an automated capacity test can be triggered on a schedule, on infrastructure changes, or before a traffic-spike event, and its results feed into a capacity model that predicts how many replicas are needed at a target RPS.

A capacity test differs from a load test in its goal: a load test validates that the system meets its SLA at an expected traffic level; a capacity test discovers the ceiling — the maximum traffic level the system can sustain before the SLA breaks. Capacity tests typically use a step-load pattern: the load is increased in discrete increments (for example, 5 RPS per step), each held for a soak period sufficient to reveal steady-state behaviour, until a saturation condition is observed (error rate exceeds 1%, or p95 latency exceeds the SLA threshold).

Automated reporting converts the raw time-series data from the capacity test into a structured output: the breaking point (traffic level at which the SLA breaks), the capacity model (a table or curve of replica count versus sustainable RPS), trend charts comparing the current run against previous runs, and a pass/fail summary that can gate a CI pipeline.
Why it matters

Agentic AI applications have non-linear capacity curves. Unlike conventional APIs where throughput scales roughly linearly with replica count, agentic systems are bounded by the external LLM API rate limits and by the combinatorial effect of concurrent LLM calls per user session. Adding more application server replicas does not increase throughput once the LLM provider's concurrent request cap is reached. Capacity tests reveal this ceiling explicitly.

Without automated capacity testing, capacity planning is either too conservative (over-provisioning expensive GPU or high-tier API keys) or too aggressive (under-provisioning and discovering limits during a production traffic spike). Automated tests running on a monthly schedule catch capacity drift — the gradual erosion of headroom caused by increased prompt length, additional tool calls, or slower upstream tool services — before it becomes a production problem.
Benefits
Provides an objective, reproducible answer to the question: how many replicas do we need to handle N requests per second?
Catches capacity drift: monthly runs detect gradual headroom erosion caused by prompt changes or upstream slowdowns.
Enables infrastructure cost optimization: accurate capacity models prevent both over-provisioning and under-provisioning.
Generates a structured report that communicates performance data to non-technical stakeholders (product, finance, operations).
Identifies the bottleneck component: capacity tests with per-component instrumentation reveal whether the ceiling is application CPU, connection pool, LLM API rate limit, or tool service throughput.
CI integration creates an automatic record of capacity test history linked to code versions.
Trade-offs
Full capacity tests are expensive: running step loads up to the breaking point consumes significant API tokens and compute time.
Capacity test results are environment-specific: a test on a 4-replica staging environment does not directly predict production capacity.
The breaking point detection logic requires careful threshold tuning — too sensitive and normal variance triggers false positives; too loose and genuine regressions are missed.
Automated environment provisioning (Terraform, Kubernetes scaling) adds engineering complexity to the test harness.
Capacity models become stale when the LLM provider changes their rate limits, when tool service SLAs change, or when the average token count per request shifts significantly.
How it works
Step-load test pattern

A step-load test starts at a low, known-safe traffic level and increases the load in fixed increments. At each step, the load is held constant for a soak period (typically 2–5 minutes) to allow the system to reach steady state. Metrics are collected during the last minute of each soak period — the first minute may include transient spikes as the load increases. The test terminates when the error rate exceeds the configured threshold or when the p95 latency exceeds the SLA ceiling.
Breaking point detection

Breaking point detection logic compares the most recent soak period's metrics against the configured thresholds: if error rate exceeds 1% or p95 latency exceeds the SLA ceiling, the capacity ceiling has been found. The last step that remained within thresholds is recorded as the sustainable capacity at the current replica count. This data point feeds the capacity model.
Capacity model construction

Running step-load tests at multiple replica counts (2, 4, 8) produces a capacity model: a mapping of replica count to sustainable RPS. This model is used for infrastructure sizing decisions. For agentic AI systems, the capacity model typically shows sublinear scaling — doubling replicas does not double sustainable RPS because the LLM API rate limit is the binding constraint beyond a certain point.
Report generation

After the test run, the automation script generates an HTML report with latency percentile time-series charts, a capacity model table, a comparison against the previous run's results, and a pass/fail summary. The report is published as a CI artifact and optionally pushed to a team dashboard. A JSON summary is also generated for downstream processing by capacity planning tools.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Capacity test automation workflow: define targets, provision environment, execute step-load test, collect metrics, generate capacity model report

content.text: The diagram shows the five-stage capacity test automation pipeline. The Define stage sets RPS targets, token budgets, and SLA thresholds. The Provision stage scales the infrastructure to the test configuration. The Execute stage runs the step-load pattern. The Collect stage aggregates latency, error rate, and resource utilization metrics. The Report stage generates the capacity model table and trend charts.
When to use it
Run automated capacity tests monthly to detect capacity drift caused by incremental changes.
Run them before scaling events (product launches, planned traffic spikes) to validate that provisioned capacity is sufficient.
Run them after significant infrastructure changes (new replica count, new connection pool size, new API key tier).
Run them after significant prompt or model changes that could alter average token counts per request.
Do not run full capacity tests in production; use a pre-production environment that mirrors production configuration.
How to implement

# scripts/capacity_test.py — automated capacity test with step load

import asyncio
import time
import json
import statistics
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from pathlib import Path
import httpx

@dataclass
class StepResult:
    rps: float
    p50_ms: float
    p95_ms: float
    p99_ms: float
    error_rate_pct: float
    within_sla: bool

@dataclass  
class CapacityTestResult:
    timestamp: str
    config: dict
    steps: list = field(default_factory=list)
    ceiling_rps: float = None
    passed: bool = False

STEP_SIZES = [5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30]  # RPS levels to test
SOAK_DURATION_S = 120   # seconds per step
WARMUP_DURATION_S = 30  # initial warmup excluded from metrics
SLA_P95_MS = 10000
SLA_ERROR_RATE_PCT = 1.0
TARGET_URL = "http://staging-api.internal/api/v1/chat"

QUERIES = [
    {"message": "Summarize the key findings of the attached document."},
    {"message": "What are the main risks in the project plan?"},
    {"message": "Draft a reply to this customer inquiry."},
    {"message": "List the top five optimization opportunities."},
]

async def run_soak(rps: float, duration_s: int) -> StepResult:
    latencies = []
    errors = 0
    total = 0
    delay = 1.0 / rps
    
    start_time = time.monotonic()
    tasks = []
    
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client:
        while time.monotonic() - start_time < duration_s:
            query = QUERIES[total % len(QUERIES)]
            t0 = time.monotonic()
            try:
                resp = await client.post(TARGET_URL, json=query)
                elapsed = (time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000
                if resp.status_code == 200:
                    latencies.append(elapsed)
                else:
                    errors += 1
            except Exception:
                errors += 1
            total += 1
            await asyncio.sleep(delay)
    
    if not latencies:
        return StepResult(rps, 0, 0, 0, 100.0, False)
    
    latencies.sort()
    n = len(latencies)
    return StepResult(
        rps=rps,
        p50_ms=latencies[n // 2],
        p95_ms=latencies[int(n * 0.95)],
        p99_ms=latencies[int(n * 0.99)],
        error_rate_pct=errors / total * 100,
        within_sla=(latencies[int(n * 0.95)] < SLA_P95_MS and errors / total * 100 < SLA_ERROR_RATE_PCT),
    )

async def run_capacity_test() -> CapacityTestResult:
    from datetime import datetime, timezone
    result = CapacityTestResult(
        timestamp=datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        config={
            "step_sizes_rps": STEP_SIZES,
            "sla_p95_ms": SLA_P95_MS,
            "sla_error_rate_pct": SLA_ERROR_RATE_PCT,
        }
    )
    
    for rps in STEP_SIZES:
        print(f"  Testing at {rps} RPS (warmup {WARMUP_DURATION_S}s + soak {SOAK_DURATION_S}s)...")
        await run_soak(rps, WARMUP_DURATION_S)  # warmup, results discarded
        step = await run_soak(rps, SOAK_DURATION_S)
        result.steps.append(asdict(step))
        
        print(f"    p95={step.p95_ms:.0f}ms  errors={step.error_rate_pct:.2f}%  within_sla={step.within_sla}")
        
        if not step.within_sla:
            result.ceiling_rps = STEP_SIZES[STEP_SIZES.index(rps) - 1] if rps != STEP_SIZES[0] else 0
            print(f"  SLA breached at {rps} RPS. Ceiling: {result.ceiling_rps} RPS.")
            break
    else:
        result.ceiling_rps = STEP_SIZES[-1]
    
    result.passed = result.ceiling_rps is not None and result.ceiling_rps >= STEP_SIZES[-2]
    return result

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    result = asyncio.run(run_capacity_test())
    Path("capacity_test_result.json").write_text(json.dumps(asdict(result), indent=2))
    print(f"\nCapacity ceiling: {result.ceiling_rps} RPS. Passed: {result.passed}")
    sys.exit(0 if result.passed else 1)

# scripts/generate_capacity_report.py — generate HTML report from capacity test results import json from pathlib import Path from datetime import datetime def generate_html_report(current_path: str, previous_path: str = None) -> str: current = json.loads(Path(current_path).read_text()) prev = json.loads(Path(previous_path).read_text()) if previous_path and Path(previous_path).exists() else None rows = "" for step in current["steps"]: sla_class = "color:green" if step["within_sla"] else "color:red" prev_p95 = None if prev: prev_steps = {s["rps"]: s for s in prev["steps"]} if step["rps"] in prev_steps: prev_p95 = prev_steps[step["rps"]]["p95_ms"] delta = f" ({'+' if prev_p95 and step['p95_ms'] > prev_p95 else ''}{step['p95_ms'] - prev_p95:.0f}ms vs prev)" if prev_p95 else "" rows += f""" {step['rps']} RPS {step['p50_ms']:.0f}ms {step['p95_ms']:.0f}ms{delta} {step['p99_ms']:.0f}ms {step['error_rate_pct']:.2f}% {'PASS' if step['within_sla'] else 'FAIL'} """ ceiling = current.get("ceiling_rps", "N/A") timestamp = current.get("timestamp", "unknown") html = f""" Capacity Test Report

Capacity Test Report

Run: {timestamp} | Ceiling: {ceiling} RPS | Status: {'PASSED' if current.get('passed') else 'FAILED'}

{rows}
Loadp50p95p99Error RateSLA
""" return html if __name__ == "__main__": import sys html = generate_html_report("capacity_test_result.json", "capacity_test_result_prev.json") Path("capacity_report.html").write_text(html) print("Report written to capacity_report.html")
Examples

# .github/workflows/monthly-capacity-test.yml
name: Monthly Capacity Test
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 3 1 * *"  # 3 AM UTC on the 1st of each month
  workflow_dispatch:
jobs:
  capacity-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 120
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install -e ".[test]"
      
      - name: Fetch previous result for comparison
        run: |
          aws s3 cp s3://capacity-test-results/latest.json capacity_test_result_prev.json || true
        env:
          AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
          AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
      
      - name: Run capacity test (4 replicas)
        run: python scripts/capacity_test.py
        env:
          OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.CAPACITY_TEST_API_KEY }}
      
      - name: Generate HTML report
        run: python scripts/generate_capacity_report.py
      
      - name: Store results in S3
        run: |
          aws s3 cp capacity_test_result.json s3://capacity-test-results/$(date +%Y%m%d).json
          aws s3 cp capacity_test_result.json s3://capacity-test-results/latest.json
          aws s3 cp capacity_report.html s3://capacity-test-results/report-$(date +%Y%m%d).html
      
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: capacity-test-report
          path: |
            capacity_test_result.json
            capacity_report.html
      
      - name: Notify Slack if ceiling below threshold
        if: failure()
        run: |
          curl -X POST ${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }} \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d '{"text": "Capacity test failed. Ceiling may have regressed. See CI artifacts."}'
Real-world example

A customer service AI platform runs monthly capacity tests on a 4-replica staging environment. The test revealed that the sustainable capacity was 18 RPS with the original single-LLM-call-per-request design. After introducing a second LLM call for response quality checking, the capacity dropped to 10 RPS — a 44% reduction. The capacity report comparison chart made this regression immediately visible, and the team triaged the quality-check step to run asynchronously (post-response delivery) to restore the capacity while retaining the quality improvement.

The team also discovered through capacity tests that the bottleneck shifted between replica counts. At 2 replicas, the bottleneck was application-server CPU. At 4 replicas, it shifted to the LLM API rate limit (RPM cap on the team's API key tier). At 8 replicas, adding more replicas produced no additional throughput — the capacity was capped by the RPM limit. This analysis justified upgrading to a higher API tier for the 4-replica configuration instead of doubling hardware.
Common mistakes
Not running a warmup period before collecting soak metrics — transient startup behaviour skews the results.
Using too short a soak period (under 60 seconds) — insufficient time to reveal steady-state behaviour, especially for connection pool saturation.
Not comparing current results against previous runs — a single run provides a snapshot, not a regression signal.
Running capacity tests against a production environment — the test traffic interferes with real users.
Ignoring the LLM API rate limit as a capacity constraint — tests that do not model rate limiting will produce optimistic results.
Not capturing per-replica resource utilization — CPU, memory, and connection pool metrics are necessary to identify whether the bottleneck is infrastructure or upstream APIs.
Best practices
Run capacity tests at multiple replica counts in a single automated test matrix to build the capacity model in one pipeline run.
Include a pre-test baseline check (a single request at nominal load) to confirm the environment is healthy before starting the step load.
Store all results in a versioned artifact store with a retention policy that keeps at least 12 months of history.
Set a minimum acceptable ceiling RPS in the CI configuration; if the measured ceiling falls below this value, fail the pipeline.
Include resource utilization metrics (CPU, memory, connection count) in the report alongside latency and error rate.
Generate a projected capacity model that forecasts headroom at expected peak traffic levels (1x, 2x, 5x current baseline).
Performance & scalability

Capacity test automation scripts must themselves be performant. The load generator must be able to sustain the target RPS without becoming the bottleneck. Python asyncio-based generators using httpx can typically sustain 20–50 RPS on a single runner; for higher rates, distribute the load across multiple runner instances or use a dedicated load testing tool (Locust distributed mode, k6 cloud).

The report generation step should process test data incrementally as results arrive, not batch-process all data at the end. For a 30-minute test run with per-second metric sampling, the raw data set is manageable in memory; for longer tests or higher sampling rates, stream results to a time-series database (InfluxDB, Prometheus remote write) during execution.
Tooling & ecosystem
Locust
: Python load testing framework; distributed mode allows multi-runner capacity tests above 50 RPS.
k6
: JavaScript load testing with built-in cloud execution; supports step-load scenarios natively via stages.
Terraform
: infrastructure provisioning for capacity test environments; creates reproducible multi-replica configurations.
Kubernetes HPA
: used to provision the exact replica count for each capacity test scenario.
InfluxDB + Grafana
: time-series storage and dashboarding for capacity test metric history.
AWS S3 / GCS
: artifact stores for capacity test result JSON files and HTML reports.

Security & reliability

Capacity test environments must use isolated infrastructure and dedicated API keys with spending caps. A capacity test that discovers a higher ceiling than expected may consume significantly more tokens than planned. Set a hard spending cap on the capacity test API key (for example, $200 per run) and configure the test script to abort if the cap is approaching.

Capacity test result artifacts contain system performance data that could reveal infrastructure topology and bottleneck information. Store them in access-controlled artifact repositories, not in public CI artifact stores. HTML reports shared with stakeholders should be sanitized to remove internal hostnames and rate limit configuration details.
Key takeaways
Capacity tests use a step-load pattern to find the breaking point — the traffic level at which the SLA breaks — rather than validating a known-good traffic level.
Running step-load tests at multiple replica counts produces a capacity model that informs infrastructure sizing decisions.
Automated monthly capacity tests catch capacity drift caused by incremental changes to prompt length, tool calls, and upstream service latency.
The LLM API rate limit is typically the binding constraint beyond a certain replica count; doubling replicas does not double capacity once this ceiling is reached.
Report comparison against previous runs is the primary mechanism for detecting capacity regressions over time.
Spending caps on capacity test API keys are mandatory to prevent runaway token costs from tests that find a higher ceiling than expected.
Infrastructure bottleneck identification (application CPU vs. connection pool vs. API rate limit) requires per-component resource utilization metrics in the report.
